data.title: Changes to fire services law with tougher penalties set for public consultation
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities are planning to launch a public consultation over a raft of new fire safety-related requirements and offences with tough penalties following the city’s deadliest blaze in decades.
Confirming an earlier report by the South China Morning Post, the Security Bureau on Friday laid out its planned amendments to the Fire Services Ordinance, including a requirement that fire alarms be checked every six months.
The bureau said its public consultation would follow its meeting with the Legislative Council’s security panel, scheduled for May 5.
“Our goal is to submit the bill to the Legislative Council for consideration by the end of 2026, with the aim of implementing various reform measures as soon as possible,” it said.
The bureau said that one of the key revisions it was proposing was elevating appropriate administrative measures to legal requirements, such as conducting alarm bell tests at least every six months.
Other proposed changes previously reported by SCMP include sharply increasing the maximum penalty for existing offences related to unauthorised handling of fire equipment and the issuance of false or misleading certificates, up from a HK$50,000 (US$6,380) fine to a maximum fine of HK$3 million and three years’ imprisonment.

The amendments would also require prior approval be obtained before firefighting systems were deactivated, such as hosepipe reels and alarms.
Under existing regulations, contractors are only required to submit a shutdown notice to authorities after deactivating such systems.
Contractors would also be in breach of regulations if they failed to notify the Fire Services Department of any malfunctioning firefighting systems within 24 hours, display annual fire inspection certificates or implement temporary fire safety measures.
Another new offence will target anyone who deliberately or recklessly misuses or interferes with fire equipment, with offenders facing a fine of HK$1 million and a one-year jail term.
But for serious cases resulting in severe casualties and property losses, the offence will carry a maximum penalty of three years’ imprisonment and a fine of HK$3 million.
Hearings by a judge-led independent inquiry into last November’s deadly blaze at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court exposed a series of failures and alleged malpractice by various parties, which led to the deactivation of fire alarms during a renovation project.

data.title: US crackdown threat could shake out China’s ‘distillation’ AI copycats: analysts
data.description:
The administration of Donald Trump has threatened action to shield the US artificial intelligence industry from being “distilled” by Chinese rivals, in a move analysts say could weed out weaker players in China’s AI sector within a year.
In a memo released on Thursday, Michael Kratsios, the science and technology adviser to the US president, warned that “surreptitious, unauthorised distillation campaigns” were enabling foreign entities – mainly in China – to release models that appear to match leading systems on select benchmarks at a fraction of the cost.
“Distillation” was a widely used technique in which a smaller “student” model was trained on the outputs of a more advanced “teacher” model, allowing developers to replicate capabilities more cheaply, said Helen Toner, interim executive director at Georgetown University’s Centre for Security and Emerging Technology, during testimony before the Senate on Wednesday.
Some Chinese start-ups had claimed to “self-develop” models while relying heavily on distillation, and such firms lacking original research could be “forced out of the game” within six to 12 months, said Zhang Ruiwang, a Beijing-based information systems architect.
Even among more capable developers, distillation is often used to accelerate iteration. Zhang said this could lengthen development cycles: gaps that might previously have been filled within three months could now take a year or more.
Allegations surrounding the practice have surfaced repeatedly in recent months. Toner, a former board member of OpenAI, told a US Senate hearing this week there was “strong evidence” that Chinese AI firms were using distillation techniques to extract capabilities from US models.

However, she cautioned against overstating its role. “Chinese AI scientists and engineers are genuinely innovative,” Toner said, adding that “making policy on the assumption that Chinese advances rest solely on theft would be a mistake”.
Chinese tech analysts share that view. Chinese AI models were unlikely to have reached their current performance levels through distillation alone – which is just one tool rather than a full explanation – as advances still relied on data engineering, infrastructure and optimisation, according to Poe Zhao, founder of Hello China Tech.
The claims from Washington have drawn pushback from Beijing. Distillation was “standard practice in the global AI sector”, the Communist Party’s official newspaper People’s Daily said on Friday, questioning why its use by Chinese firms should be labelled as theft.
The Chinese embassy in Washington echoed that view, with spokesman Liu Pengyu saying such accusations amounted to unwarranted pressure, while reiterating Beijing’s support for technological development through fair competition and international cooperation.
Some researchers have also urged caution. Nathan Lambert of the Allen Institute for AI said on X that it remained unclear how much Chinese firms materially benefited from distillation, warning that sweeping restrictions on open-weight models could ultimately slow US innovation and weaken the broader research ecosystem.
In recent months, OpenAI, Google and Anthropic have all accused Chinese rivals of carrying out so-called “distillation attacks”.
While the technique itself is not illegal, its growing use is sharpening regulatory tensions. “Distillation, as a technical approach, is not inherently unlawful,” law firm King & Wood Mallesons said in a report this month. “However, as competitors rapidly close the gap within existing frameworks, leading firms have stronger incentives to push for tighter regulation.”
Chinese foreign ministry spokesman Guo Jiakun said on Friday that the US allegations were “groundless” and were aimed at smearing Chinese AI achievements.
Additional reporting by Ben Jiang

data.title: Disciplinary inquiry resumes into doctor’s alleged baby blunder 16 years ago
data.description:
Hong Kong’s Medical Council resumed a disciplinary inquiry on Friday over a suspected medical blunder that left a boy permanently disabled more than 16 years ago after its decision to terminate the hearing sparked a public outcry.
Dr Sit Sou-chi was accused of failing to carry out all necessary and immediate investigations on newborn Li Yuanjian, the son of a mainland Chinese couple, after the infant had a seizure on December 22, 2009.
On Friday, Sit’s counsel grilled nurse Ho Kit-ha, who was on duty that night, as well as a doctor who served as an expert witness.
The two, along with Hui Shuk-kuen, were the witnesses from the Medical Council’s side testifying during the hearing that lasted for more than eight hours.
Parents Li Zhijian and Peng Hongying also attended the hearing.
“I just hope there will be a judgment on Sunday,” Li told the media after the hearing adjourned. “I don’t want to delay this matter any longer.”
The inquiry on Friday heard that Ho first called Sit at 4.20am, almost an hour after she noticed the baby suffering from suspected convulsions at 3.30am.
Ho then noticed a few instances of abnormalities after 6am, but Sit was only contacted again at 7.30am.

Defence counsel Anthony Ismail asked Ho why she did not notify Sit soon after she noticed the abnormalities early in the morning.
Ho replied that she believed Sit, who had told her he would arrive at the hospital earlier to check the baby.
She said she was also handling the baby as well as other infants in the ward, and Yuanjian did not display abnormalities shortly after.
Ismail also asked Ho if she recalled Sit asking her to record hourly observations of the baby’s vital signs, including respiration rates. Ho said such monitoring was a protocol nurses followed anyway.
Dr Alan So King-woon, an expert witness and a consultant at the department of paediatrics at Prince of Wales Hospital, said neonatal seizures were difficult to diagnose.
Doctors could not rely solely on a nurse’s observations and had a responsibility to investigate other symptoms, he said.
So said any suspicions made it necessary to go back to the newborn and perform further physical examination.
Ismail then asked So if it was reasonable for Ho to have contacted Sit almost an hour after she noticed the suspected convulsions at 3.30am.
The expert witness said it was “not unreasonable”, given nurses needed time to stabilise the baby and manage other newborns in the ward.
The inquiry panel was led by council chairwoman Grace Tang Wai-king.
The boy was born at Baptist Hospital in Kowloon Tong on December 19, 2009.
Results of prenatal check-ups at the private hospital appeared normal, Peng said in an interview with the South China Morning Post last year.
However, the couple said no one had informed them about their son’s medical condition on December 22 until about 8am that day, when he had been unwell, placed in an incubator and suffered three convulsions.
The incident left their son with cerebral palsy and quadriplegia, unable to care for himself for life.
The inquiry was initially scheduled for 2016 but was postponed at Sit’s request to review new evidence until the council terminated it last year, saying the prolongation had made the hearing unfair to the doctor.
The case sparked a public outcry and prompted the government to amend the relevant laws to enhance the effectiveness of complaint handling. The council also later overturned its decision to terminate the hearing.
The hearing will resume Sunday.

data.title: Huawei, DeepSeek strengthen China’s AI self-reliance with V4 collaboration
data.description:
Huawei Technologies’ newest Ascend 950PR and 950DT chips enjoyed “day zero” adaptation to DeepSeek’s latest V4 model, the Shenzhen-based tech giant said in a livestream on Friday, just hours after the model’s release.
The hardware-software collaboration between the two Chinese firms underscores the progress China has made in tech self-reliance, a top national priority for Beijing amid US efforts to block its access to advanced semiconductor chips and chipmaking equipment.
During the livestream hosted on Bilibili and WeChat, Huawei engineers outlined the adaptation of its chips and flagship software system Compute Architecture for Neural Networks (CANN) – its equivalent to Nvidia’s dominant CUDA software – with DeepSeek’s latest model.
The company said its entire Ascend SuperNode product line was “fully adapted” to DeepSeek V4 for model inference, which had “significantly improved” due to the two companies’ close collaboration before the model’s release.
Inference refers to the serving and running of an AI model. While cutting-edge Chinese models are still reliant on advanced US chips for training, computational demand for inference was set to surpass training demand globally by 2030, according to McKinsey & Company.
Huawei’s next-generation Ascend 950PR and 950DT chips are expected to be available by the end of the year. In its announcement on Friday, DeepSeek said its V4 model would have throughput issues until the second half of the year, which was when Huawei’s Ascend 950PR supernodes would “ship at scale”.

Phelix Lee, senior equity analyst at Morningstar, said the adaptation of Ascend and other domestic chips with DeepSeek V4 reflected the broader trend of Chinese AI model companies switching from foreign chips to domestic replacements.
Besides Huawei, Chinese chip designers Moore Threads and Cambricon Technologies also announced on Friday that their respective chips enjoyed “day zero” adaptation with V4.
Meanwhile, Tencent Holdings was one of the first domestic cloud service providers to make V4 available on its “TokenHub” platform, which it said was powered by chips from multiple domestic chipmakers.
These developments bode well for China’s entire domestic chip supply chain, according to Lee, who added that not only foundries would benefit, but also domestic chip equipment vendors such as SMEE and Naura.
“The biggest [uncertainty] … is whether this increase in domestic AI demand will be profitable in the next two or three years amid elevated capital expenditures and low yields,” he said.
DeepSeek’s new model comes as the US mulls expanding export controls on China, including a package of 20 new bills that advanced through a key congressional committee on Wednesday.
While US restrictions on access to advanced chips from the likes of Nvidia and AMD have slowed Chinese AI model development, they have also galvanised the domestic chip industry to step up to fill the gap.
Though DeepSeek did not disclose whether it trained its latest model on Nvidia chips, as it has done previously, domestic rivals are actively experimenting with using domestic chips for model training as they look to reduce their reliance on US chips.
On Friday, Chinese on-demand services group Meituan invited users to test a new trillion-parameter AI model, which local reports said was trained entirely on domestically produced chips.
Meituan did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Ivan Su, senior equity analyst at Morningstar, said equity markets were unlikely to react as they did last year when DeepSeek released its groundbreaking R1 model, triggering a sell-off that saw Nvidia shed close to US$600 billion in market capitalisation in a single day.
“V4 is simply a follow-through on that same trend, and trends don’t make headlines the way shocks do,” Su said, adding that the “reality” of the competitiveness and cost efficiency of Chinese AI had already been priced in.
Still, a situation where DeepSeek’s newest models were optimised to run on Huawei chips would be “a horrible outcome” for the US, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said on a podcast last week.

Simon Willison, a prominent AI blogger, wrote on Friday that the new DeepSeek model was “the cheapest of the larger frontier models” globally.
In an extended technical report accompanying the new model, DeepSeek said V4 was competitive overall with leading closed-source US models, while being “approximately 3 to 6 months” behind on reasoning capabilities.
Benjamin Pou, Hong Kong-based founder of coding education company KTBYTE, said the rapid improvement of open-source Chinese models in recent months had made it “uneconomical” to use expensive US models for customer-facing services, particularly as the rise of AI agents greatly increased AI token usage.
“We should expect AI agents to be cheap and universal in the future just like web pages and social media are democratised today,” he said.
Additional reporting by Xinmei Shen

data.title: Departments must communicate better, fire chief tells Tai Po blaze hearing
data.description:
Hong Kong’s fire services chief has acknowledged that better communication with building authorities is needed after an independent committee earlier revealed that his officers had taken no action against possible safety breaches during renovations at Wang Fuk Court, where 168 people were killed in last year’s inferno.
But Director of Fire Services Andy Yeung Yan-kin insisted on Friday that authorities had clear divisions of work when it came to the supervision of renovation projects, even as the use of flammable materials at the estate in Tai Po had gone unregulated before the blaze.
“We’ll take action against polyfoam boards and temporary openings [at evacuation routes], but the work is divided,” he told the independent committee investigating the disaster. “We know what we’re doing.”
He said building authorities, including the Buildings Department and the Housing Bureau’s Independent Checking Unit, were responsible for overseeing building structural safety, whereas the Fire Services Department monitored the operation of fire installations.
“This is very clear. If we interfere, our roles may overlap,” he added.
Mr Justice David Lok Kai-hong, the committee’s chairman, pointed out that the Buildings Department might not be able to identify all fire hazards at construction sites.
“This incident highlights the necessity for better communication,” the judge said, with Yeung agreeing.




The committee was scrutinising authorities’ inability to detect the failures of fire prevention measures at the residential complex during a HK$336 million (US$42.9 million) renovation project that began in 2024.
Previous sessions revealed that fire officers had taken no action against possible breaches, including the use of polyfoam boards to seal windows and the replacement of fireproof windows with movable wooden boards to allow workers access to scaffolding.
An assistant fire services director said on Wednesday that his department lacked the construction expertise to decide which items or arrangements were integral to renovation works, instead deferring enforcement decisions to building authorities.
The committee’s leading senior counsel, Victor Dawes, pointed out on Friday that the department was ultimately responsible for identifying fire hazards and taking action to minimise risks.
He cited a 2020 High Court judgment on a legal challenge arising from the department’s requests for a storage facility to revise its layout following a deadly fire in Ngau Tau Kok in 2016.
In finding the requests justified, Mr Justice Wilson Chan Ka-shun said the department had a statutory duty to advise on fire protection measures, although other government bodies also had a role to play.
Yeung agreed that the department was empowered to issue fire hazards abatement notices at construction sites, but he said building authorities would also consider structural details when enforcing the law.
The director noted that the Buildings Department updated its code of practice last month to ban the use of polyfoam materials on the outer walls of buildings, but he noted exceptions could apply to those with fire-resistant capabilities.
He said his department had advised the Buildings Department on law enforcement after the latest fire, but acknowledged the need for the two organisations to clarify the applicable standards.
“Different departments have to complement each other to ensure fire safety,” Yeung said. “As much as we may not fully understand the structural specifications of windows, the Buildings Department may not realise the firefighting difficulties arising from a change in window structures.”
The committee also examined a four-year delay of an infrastructure upgrade for the Fire Services Department’s emergency call centre, after its ability to handle an influx of calls in a disaster was called into question.
Previous sessions revealed the department’s call centre had only 30 telephone lines in operation, in contrast to police’s 200.

Security chief Chris Tang Ping-keung said last week the department would have 18 new lines soon, with another 18 to 20 in the following months. The total would eventually increase to 100 by early next year after a command system upgrade.
Dawes pointed out that authorities had planned to upgrade the department’s communication facilities as early as 2017, with the new system initially expected to go into use in 2022.
Yeung blamed the 2019 anti-government protests and the Covid-19 pandemic for causing a delay in the system’s delivery.
Regarding the handling of cases, the director said the use of artificial intelligence to screen out duplicate calls had to be viewed with caution, warning that filtering out original messages by mistake could lead to delayed rescues.
The committee heard that the inferno, which raged across seven of eight residential blocks for about 43 hours, presented unprecedented challenges to firefighting and rescue efforts.
Tang Wing-wah, an assistant director of the department who oversaw the operation as incident commander between 4.18pm and 6.22pm on November 26, said falling debris and scaffolding had prevented turntable ladders from getting close to the buildings.
The estate-wide deactivation of booster pumps for the renovation project forced firefighters to carry portable pumps weighing 80kg (176lbs) each to increase the water pressure for combating the blaze at higher levels, he said.
Tang revealed the department at one point considered having firefighters break into the 10th floor of Wang Tai House, the second building that caught fire, via turntable ladders, but eventually dropped the idea due to the intensity of the blaze at the time.
He stressed that commanding officers could exercise their powers flexibly and request extra resources from the fire services communication centre without first seeking his consent, as they knew best what resources were required on the ground.
The hearing continues at City Gallery in Central on Monday.

data.title: Why China’s optical communications sector is the latest AI boom beneficiary
data.description:
Amid the artificial intelligence boom, the world has been laser-focused on technologies like graphics processing units and memory chips. However, a quieter miracle has been unfolding in the mainland stock market.
Optical modules, which allow ultra-fast communications in data centres, have minted a new class of industrial upstarts.
In the past year, shares in Shenzhen-listed Zhongji Innolight, the world’s largest optical module producer, jumped tenfold. Smaller peers Eoptolink Technology and Suzhou TFC Optical Communication, also listed in Shenzhen, notched share-price gains of more than five times. And the combined market capitalisation of the three firms recently eclipsed that of baijiu distiller Kweichow Moutai – the long-standing champion of Chinese equities.
In the first quarter, Innolight and Eoptolink were the second and third favourite stocks among actively managed equity funds in the country, trailing only the world’s leading maker of electric-vehicle batteries, Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd (CATL), according to financial information provider Wind. TFC ranked 12th, ahead of Ping An Insurance and chipmaker Cambricon Technologies.
Notably, Innolight and TFC have both recently announced plans to list in Hong Kong, further expanding their influence and footprint overseas.
What do the firms make?
Optical modules, also known as optical transceivers, convert electrical signals to optical signals, and vice versa, for high-speed data transmission in networking and AI infrastructure systems.
Data centres are increasingly shifting from copper wires to optical interconnect systems, because only the latter – using light pulses travelling through glass-fibre cables – can provide the massive bandwidth that AI and machine learning set-ups require.
As AI clusters continued to expand, there had been a “significant increase in demand for optical transceiver modules” capable of transmission speeds of 800 gigabits per second or higher, according to a report this week by TrendForce.
All three firms provide products for that speed, as well as more powerful modules capable of 1.6 terabits per second – required by Nvidia’s latest-generation Rubin platform and Blackwell products.
Meanwhile, the companies have also expanded to a next-generation technology known as co-packaged optics (CPO), which places optical devices inside the same chip package as semiconductors known as application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs). Putting the formerly separate devices in closer proximity slashes power consumption, increases bandwidth density and reduces latency.
Why are optical modules in high demand?
The demand for high-speed connectivity is increasing rapidly. The average capacity of hyperscale data centres to be opened by 2028 would be almost double that of the facilities operating in 2025, while the total capacity of all operational hyperscale data centres was set to grow almost threefold by 2030, according to Synergy Research Group.
Massive cloud computing is driving the evolution of data centre network architectures, from the traditional three-tier design to a so-called spine-leaf architecture, a two-layer network topology designed for high-speed, low-latency communication. This change would increase the number of optical modules required as much as tenfold, according to an Eastmoney Securities report earlier this week.

This explosive demand is reflected in the companies’ latest financial reports. Innolight nearly tripled its revenue in the first quarter, while profit jumped by more than 260 per cent. Eoptolink more than doubled its revenue in the same period, while TFC’s sales rose more than 40 per cent.
Where does China stand?
Chinese firms now dominate a global top 10 list of transceiver manufacturers compiled by industry intelligence firm LightCounting. Innolight topped the most recent list in 2024, with Eoptolink in third and Huawei Technologies, Accenlink, Hisense and HG Genuine all making the cut.
Innolight did not appear in the top 10 in 2010, but climbed to second place in just eight years, trailing only Finisar, an American optical giant acquired by Coherent in 2019. Coherent ranked second in the 2024 list.
The Chinese firms are not just domestic players; they are primary suppliers to Western tech giants. For example, Innolight’s major clients included Nvidia, Google and Amazon, according to Eastmoney Securities.
Earlier this week, TFC announced a joint venture in Singapore with AI infrastructure solutions provider SuperX AI Technology to focus on optical interconnect products for the international market.

data.title: Over 50 Central restaurants, shops join drive to boost spending, lure tourists
data.description:
More than 50 restaurants and shops have joined a “tourism everywhere” initiative to promote Hong Kong’s bustling Central district among residents and tourists and boost local spending.
The Hong Kong Small and Medium Enterprises Association on Friday revealed the programme to promote hidden delicacies and goods in Central, aligning with the government’s objective to turn everywhere into a tourist destination.
The initiative runs from May 1 until the end of June, with the first five days overlapping with mainland China’s Labour Day “golden week” holiday.
The Immigration Department expects about 980,000 mainland visitors to enter Hong Kong between May 1 and 5, an increase of 7 per cent over the same period last year.
The programme will offer discounts at participating merchants to residents and tourists, aiming to encourage them to explore the art, historical sites, local culture and delicacies of the Central and Sheung Wan area.
Secretary for Commerce and Economic Development Algernon Yau Ying-wah told the launch ceremony that he expected the scheme to draw more tourists and residents.
“The initiative aims to leverage the collaboration between chambers of commerce and various stakeholders to pool resources, encourage small and medium-sized businesses in the community to offer discounts, attract more foot traffic and spending, stimulate business growth and allow residents and tourists to experience the unique characteristics of different communities and the charm of Hong Kong,” he said.

Yau said the initiative had attracted more than 100 merchants since it was first launched in 2024, with businesses reporting increased patrons and profits. Some recorded a 20 to 30 per cent increase in business, he added.
He said the programme showed that the industry and the businesses incorporated innovative elements and created selling points to boost local consumption.
“The government will continue to take various measures to support the upgrading and transformation of small and medium enterprises, and encourage the industry to constantly seek change, and explore and create opportunities,” he said.
Association president Andrew Kwok Chi-wah said the event would feature a themed travel special, showcasing the unique shops, street stories and shopping deals in Central to tourists from the mainland and overseas.
“We hope that a street, a shop and a story can become a focal point for attracting visitors,” he said.
Among those taking part in the initiative is EverShine, a cafe on Peel Street in Central.
EverShine owner Mabel Ho Mei-po said her cafe was joining the programme for the second time as she hoped it would help bring more patrons and also promote the area among tourists.
Her cafe would offer several types of coffee and tea at a discounted HK$13 each.
“I hope this will help promote Central, revitalise the community and let more people around the world know about the hub,” she said.

data.title: Cathay raises HK$2.08 billion after tapping public bond market for first time
data.description:
Cathay Pacific Airways has raised HK$2.08 billion (US$265.4 million) by selling Hong Kong dollar-denominated bonds to boost its working capital, tapping the public bond market for the first time.
Hong Kong’s flag carrier said on Friday that the bond issuance was the biggest of its kind by a locally based, non-public sector company.
The three-year bonds, which carried a fixed interest rate of 3.78 per cent, were sold mainly to professional investors such as asset managers, banks and private banks.
“By taking part in and supporting the development of its financial markets, we’re playing a part in reinforcing Hong Kong’s position as a leading international financial centre,” Rebecca Sharpe, Cathay Group’s chief financial officer, said.
She said the airline was committed to investing HK$100 billion in a new fleet, cabins, lounges and digital innovation, which would help strengthen Hong Kong’s role as an international aviation hub.
Cathay said it raised funds as part of its financing activities every year by taking into consideration a range of factors such as its overall funding needs, existing economic environment, fund source options and pricing.
“Cathay believes this is a good opportunity to raise funds in the Hong Kong dollar public bond market while further diversifying its fund sources,” the company said.
The bond issuance is part of its US$2.5 billion medium-term note programme.
Hong Kong’s bond market was busy this week, with semi-privatised rail giant the MTR Corporation selling HK$18.8 billion in green bonds publicly on Wednesday, setting a Hong Kong dollar-denominated issuance record and marking its first publicly offered bond in the local currency market.
The exercise aimed to diversify funding for a spate of projects.

data.title: China’s Pop Mart eyes expansion at home and abroad for its Pop Land theme park
data.description:
Chinese toymaker Pop Mart said it plans to develop more of its Pop Land theme parks on the mainland and overseas, and is in talks with several cities to expand beyond its sole existing venue in Beijing.
Jeffrey Hu, head of Pop Land and vice-president of Pop Mart, said on Friday that the company had established solid park operations capabilities, and planned to replicate this model whenever suitable opportunities emerged.
“We also plan to develop an integrated resort blending theme park facilities, commercial spaces and hotels when the time is right,” Hu said.
Jeff Zhang, an equity analyst at Morningstar, said opening resorts and theme parks overseas was an optimal move to diversify revenue away from toy merchandising.

“We think the upgraded theme park will boost ticket income and cross-selling of toy products for Pop Mart, but increased investments could mildly weigh on the company’s near-term profitability,” Zhang said.
“For overseas expansion, we think the next Pop Land will likely be launched in Thailand, featuring Labubu, Crybaby and other [intellectual properties] with strong local popularity.”
Pop Land, located in Beijing’s Sun Park in downtown Chaoyang district, has attracted an increasing number of visitors since opening nearly three years ago. The theme park turned profitable in its first year of operation.
The park will launch its upgraded Labubu Forest Zone, or phase 1.5 of Pop Land, on Thursday, featuring more attractions and shows, following a one-year renovation. Pop Mart said it expected the addition to help boost its overall revenue.
Last year, despite only one-third of the park being open to the public, visitor traffic rose by over 70 per cent from a year earlier. Non-family visitors and out-of-town travellers accounted for more than half of total attendance, and the site had become a popular spot for tourists in Beijing, including some overseas fans, according to the company.
“Pop toys are static by nature, while the park enables those IPs to come alive,” Hu said. “Pop Land still requires further optimisation in many areas, and we will expand it and make it more sophisticated in phase two.”
He added that Pop Land had no intention of rivalling major theme parks like Disneyland and Universal Studios – nor was it in a position to do so – adding that its key differentiation lay primarily in its intellectual property.
Shares of Hong Kong-listed Pop Mart edged up 0.6 per cent to close at HK$156.50 on Friday. The stock has lost more than half of its value since hitting a record high in August.
Even after posting strong 2025 full-year earnings, its share price plunged 23 per cent on March 25.
Chinese billionaire investor Duan Yongping recently said he viewed Pop Mart as a promising long-term investment.

data.title: Life insurance sales in Hong Kong hit record amid ‘rise of millionaires’
data.description:
Life insurance sales in Hong Kong rose 50.6 per cent to another record last year as affluent customers continued to buy policies in the city for wealth transfer, protection and medical needs.
The industry wrote HK$330.9 billion (US$42.2 billion) in new life policies in 2025, compared with HK$219 billion a year earlier, which was also a record, according to data from the Insurance Authority released on Friday.
The continuous growth of the business in recent years was due to more wealthy people setting up family offices in the city and using insurance policies to transfer wealth to the next generation, said Paul Murray, CEO of Swiss Re’s life and health business.
Hong Kong does not charge estate duty and has offered tax incentives for single family offices since 2023, which Murray cited as factors attracting wealthy families.
Hong Kong had more than 3,380 single-family offices operating in the city as of the end of 2025, an increase of about 680 over the past two years, according to a study by the Hong Kong Institute for Monetary and Financial Research.
“The rise of millionaires in Hong Kong led to an increase in family offices because of its friendly jurisdiction, rule of law and low-tax regime,” Murray said. “They found Hong Kong to be an ideal location for succession planning, and that led to increased sales of insurance policies.”
Murray added that high-net-worth policies paid out compensation quickly upon the demise of policyholders, helping family members avoid selling assets under unfavourable economic circumstances.

HSBC Life remained the top seller of new policies for the fourth year in a row, accounting for 15.5 per cent of sales, the Insurance Authority data showed.
Daisy Tsang, CEO of HSBC Life Hong Kong and Macau, said insurance sales had been particularly strong in the high-net-worth segment in Asia. The rising number of affluent customers, driven by the growing Asian economy, was expanding the pool of individuals seeking to protect wealth and plan for intergenerational transfer, she said.
A recent HSBC survey of 900 high-net-worth individuals found that 60 per cent had not yet taken part in wealth-transfer planning for their combined US$5.8 billion in wealth, representing a large population of potential new clients.
“The internationalisation of wealth planning is strengthening Hong Kong’s role as a regional hub, with deep capital markets and a strong advisory ecosystem enabling it to capture both onshore and offshore demand across Asia,” Tsang said.
“Asia’s ageing population is increasing demand for retirement and protection solutions as people live longer and spend more years outside the workforce.”

Swiss Re provided reinsurance to support direct insurers in offering these products, Murray said.
“High-net-worth insurance is a very rapid area of growth in Hong Kong and other markets in the Asia-Pacific, where it holds around 30 per cent of the wealth of global high-net-worth individuals,” he said.
Patrick Graham, CEO of Manulife Hong Kong and Macau, said mainland visitors remained a key growth driver for the Hong Kong insurance industry.
“Hong Kong’s insurance industry maintained strong momentum throughout 2025, driven by sustained demand for savings, health, protection and legacy solutions,” Graham said.
“Continued interest from both local customers and mainland Chinese visitors highlights Hong Kong’s strengths as a well-connected international financial centre and a key hub within the Greater Bay Area.”
The number of mainland visitors to the city rose 11 per cent to 37.83 million last year, accounting for 76 per cent of total tourists, according to data from the Hong Kong Tourism Board.

The Insurance Authority did not disclose statistics on sales to mainland visitors last year, as it was reviewing how to define and identify mainland visitors and other overseas visitors.
Graham said high-net-worth individuals were increasingly demanding cross-border and intergenerational wealth and legacy planning, reinforcing Hong Kong’s appeal as a preferred wealth-planning destination in Asia.
BOC Life CEO Wilson Tang said there was strong demand for retirement products as a result of the ageing population. People aged 65 and above accounted for 22 per cent of Hong Kong’s 7.5 million residents last year, which would rise to 31 per cent by 2036, according to an official forecast.
“The silver economy is providing new business opportunities for insurers,” Tang said. “BOC Life has provided retirement products to meet the needs of customers who want to retire and live in the Greater Bay Area and other mainland cities.”

data.title: Life insurance sales in Hong Kong hit record amid ‘rise of millionaires’
data.description:
Life insurance sales in Hong Kong rose 50.6 per cent to another record last year as affluent customers continued to buy policies in the city for wealth transfer, protection and medical needs.
The industry wrote HK$330.9 billion (US$42.2 billion) in new life policies in 2025, compared with HK$219 billion a year earlier, which was also a record, according to data from the Insurance Authority released on Friday.
The continuous growth of the business in recent years was due to more wealthy people setting up family offices in the city and using insurance policies to transfer wealth to the next generation, said Paul Murray, CEO of Swiss Re’s life and health business.
Hong Kong does not charge estate duty and has offered tax incentives for single family offices since 2023, which Murray cited as factors attracting wealthy families.
Hong Kong had more than 3,380 single-family offices operating in the city as of the end of 2025, an increase of about 680 over the past two years, according to a study by the Hong Kong Institute for Monetary and Financial Research.
“The rise of millionaires in Hong Kong led to an increase in family offices because of its friendly jurisdiction, rule of law and low-tax regime,” Murray said. “They found Hong Kong to be an ideal location for succession planning, and that led to increased sales of insurance policies.”
Murray added that high-net-worth policies paid out compensation quickly upon the demise of policyholders, helping family members avoid selling assets under unfavourable economic circumstances.

HSBC Life remained the top seller of new policies for the fourth year in a row, accounting for 15.5 per cent of sales, the Insurance Authority data showed.
Daisy Tsang, CEO of HSBC Life Hong Kong and Macau, said insurance sales had been particularly strong in the high-net-worth segment in Asia. The rising number of affluent customers, driven by the growing Asian economy, was expanding the pool of individuals seeking to protect wealth and plan for intergenerational transfer, she said.
A recent HSBC survey of 900 high-net-worth individuals found that 60 per cent had not yet taken part in wealth-transfer planning for their combined US$5.8 billion in wealth, representing a large population of potential new clients.
“The internationalisation of wealth planning is strengthening Hong Kong’s role as a regional hub, with deep capital markets and a strong advisory ecosystem enabling it to capture both onshore and offshore demand across Asia,” Tsang said.
“Asia’s ageing population is increasing demand for retirement and protection solutions as people live longer and spend more years outside the workforce.”

Swiss Re provided reinsurance to support direct insurers in offering these products, Murray said.
“High-net-worth insurance is a very rapid area of growth in Hong Kong and other markets in the Asia-Pacific, where it holds around 30 per cent of the wealth of global high-net-worth individuals,” he said.
Patrick Graham, CEO of Manulife Hong Kong and Macau, said mainland visitors remained a key growth driver for the Hong Kong insurance industry.
“Hong Kong’s insurance industry maintained strong momentum throughout 2025, driven by sustained demand for savings, health, protection and legacy solutions,” Graham said.
“Continued interest from both local customers and mainland Chinese visitors highlights Hong Kong’s strengths as a well-connected international financial centre and a key hub within the Greater Bay Area.”
The number of mainland visitors to the city rose 11 per cent to 37.83 million last year, accounting for 76 per cent of total tourists, according to data from the Hong Kong Tourism Board.

The Insurance Authority did not disclose statistics on sales to mainland visitors last year, as it was reviewing how to define and identify mainland visitors and other overseas visitors.
Graham said high-net-worth individuals were increasingly demanding cross-border and intergenerational wealth and legacy planning, reinforcing Hong Kong’s appeal as a preferred wealth-planning destination in Asia.
BOC Life CEO Wilson Tang said there was strong demand for retirement products as a result of the ageing population. People aged 65 and above accounted for 22 per cent of Hong Kong’s 7.5 million residents last year, which would rise to 31 per cent by 2036, according to an official forecast.
“The silver economy is providing new business opportunities for insurers,” Tang said. “BOC Life has provided retirement products to meet the needs of customers who want to retire and live in the Greater Bay Area and other mainland cities.”

data.title: Developing|Hong Kong CityU chief Freddy Boey quits post 2 years earlier than expected
data.description:
The head of Hong Kong’s City University, Professor Freddy Boey Yin Chiang, has resigned with immediate effect for personal reasons, leaving his post two years earlier than expected.
The university expressed its gratitude to Boey, a Singaporean, for his contributions to CityU during his three years as president.
“The executive committee of the [university’s] council has appointed provost and deputy president Professor Lee Chun-sing as acting president with immediate effect to ensure continued operation. The council will provide full support to ensure stability during the transition,” it said on Friday.
It added that a global recruitment exercise for the next president would be launched shortly.
A source on the council said Boey’s five-year contract was supposed to run until 2028.
Boey, an expert in biomaterials engineering, became CityU’s fifth president in May 2023.
The academic, born in 1956, succeeded Professor Way Kuo, who led CityU for 13 years.
The Education Bureau said it respected Boey’s personal wish and decision and thanked him for his contributions to the university during his tenure.
“The recruitment of the president is part of the university’s institutional autonomy and is taken care of by the CityU Council under the authority conferred on it by the CityU Ordinance,” a bureau spokesman said.
“The government has full confidence in the CityU council in selecting the most suitable candidate for the post based on the university’s long-term interests and development needs.”
More to follow…

data.title: Huawei earmarks US$11.7b for training of autonomous-driving systems
data.description:
Adamant about retaining its runaway lead in supplying smart driving systems in China, Huawei Technologies plans to invest as much as 80 billion yuan (US$11.7 billion) over the next five years to boost computing power essential for training and testing semi-autonomous cars.
The massive capital expenditure would enhance the reliability of cars fitted with the Huawei Qiankun ADS autopilot system, as the Shenzhen-based tech giant looked to expand its customer base, said Jin Yuzhi, CEO of Huawei’s intelligent automotive solutions business unit.
In 2026 alone, Huawei would earmark 18 billion yuan for research and development in autonomous driving, he added.
“Autonomous driving is an industry requiring long-term investment,” Jin said at an event in Beijing on Thursday. “Based on our investment of 18 billion yuan in smart driving this year, we will spend more than the combined expense of all other major [autonomous driving] solution providers.”
Computing power plays a key role in improving self-driving capabilities by processing data collected during training and testing of advanced driver assistance systems (ADASs).
Huawei’s Qiankun ADS platform surpassed 10 billion kilometres of autonomous driving early this month.
Autopilot features powered by the platform are widely used in mainland China. Huawei has partnerships with 25 car brands, and Qiankun has been installed in more than 50 models. A total of 1.7 million vehicles fitted with the system have been delivered to Chinese customers.
Aside from Huawei, only US electric vehicle (EV) maker Tesla claims to have amassed as much real-world data.
Some Chinese ADAS systems have proved superior to Tesla’s Full Self-Driving (FSD) navigation software on mainland roads, according to David Zhang, general secretary of the Shanghai-based International Intelligent Vehicle Engineering Association.
However, the Chinese self-driving technologies are so far limited to the domestic market, and FSD is the front-runner internationally, he added.
Huawei supplies domestic carmakers such as Arcfox and Avatr with automotive chips, lidar (light detection and ranging) sensors and digital technologies that allow cars to be semi-autonomous and linked to the internet.
It ventured into the car industry in 2021 through a partnership with Chongqing-based carmaker Seres. Aito, an EV venture between the two companies, is now one of the bestselling premium EV brands in China.

In mid-2024, Seres agreed to pay 2.5 billion yuan to buy the Aito ­trademark and patents from Huawei, a deal that reflected Huawei’s determination to enhance its role as a supplier of vehicle systems and know-how, rather than an EV maker, according to analysts.
Huawei’s pitch to the automotive industry is that it can help its partners cut development costs, accelerate new model launches and improve efficiency in testing smart-driving systems.
Nearly all major carmakers in China are now actively developing cars with “hands off” self-driving features to gain an edge in the cutthroat market.
In December, Beijing gave the first carmakers the go-ahead to build EVs with level 3 (L3) autonomous driving capability. On an international scale from L0 to L5, an L3 autonomous vehicle can operate independently under predefined conditions, although the driver must remain ready at all times to take control if required.

data.title: Henderson’s winning bid to run harbour event space could boost Central’s access
data.description:
A winning bid involving Henderson Land Development to operate a major Hong Kong harbourfront event space has expanded the developer’s footprint in Central to three prime locations, a situation that experts have said could result in greater accessibility and more visitors from surrounding commercial properties.
Central Grand, a 50:50 joint venture formed by Henderson and concert organiser YW Company – commonly known as “Yiu Wing” – recently won the tender to operate the 3.7-hectare (9.1 acres) Central Harbourfront Event Space for five years starting July 1.
The developer already has a substantial footprint in the city’s core business district.
In addition to managing the International Financial Centre (IFC), Henderson in 2021 secured the district’s last major plot – a site dubbed “land king” – to go on sale.
The site is being developed into the Central Yards retail and office space, with the first phase expected to open in 2027 and the second to be completed in 2032.
Yiu Wing, meanwhile, manages AXA Wonderland in the West Kowloon Cultural District and AXA Dreamland at Go Park Sai Sha in the New Territories.
Discussing the tendering exercise for the Central Harbourfront Event Space, lawmaker and urban planner Andrew Lam Siu-lo said on Friday that he agreed that the new requirements for bidders would ensure near year-round access, adding that he considered the measures essential.
“If this public area could be connected to the piers and the very important pedestrian footbridge network [among Central’s commercial buildings] … it will be beneficial to the public,” he told the South China Morning Post.

Lam added that the bridges connecting the harbourfront and other tourist spots to the rest of the district were not only functional but could also serve as attractions in themselves.
The lawmaker said the area was also served by multiple ferry routes and an MTR line, in addition to having ample space for visitor coaches to stop.
The area could serve as a tourist hub, but it would need strong attractions at the event space to bring people there, he added.
“It’s a public space after all,” he said, referring to the event space. “In the past, when no events were taking place, it became more of a place that sat idle than somewhere the public could enjoy.”
Lam said he expected synergy and management efficiency as Henderson would be involved in overseeing the event space.
But the lawmaker said he remained cautious as any benefits brought to the area would depend on the approach taken by the joint venture.
“There is the flexibility [for synergy], but it’s down to operational details later on. [Central Yards] will need more time before it’s done … What would the conveniences be? No one knows yet,” he said.
“We can see on paper as to what would be inside … a bridge that is accessible at all times, and it connects with public spaces. But in reality, how would they be perceived by the general public? That is a whole different matter,” he said.

Ivan Ho Man-yiu, the Harbourfront Commission’s chairman, said he was looking forward to the joint venture running the space, given the potential of “the significant synergy”.
He compared the relationship between the event space, the IFC and Central Yards to that of shopping centre K11 Musea and the Avenue of Stars in Tsim Sha Tsui.
But he stressed the importance of making sure events held there were not too commercial and could be enjoyed by all.
“The public’s enjoyment and engagement with the harbourfront are our priorities,” Ho said.
Some clauses of the tender enable the tenant or the Development Bureau to intervene to prevent repeats of controversies such as the hot air balloon festival last September that failed to provide tethered rides as advertised.
“We also require the tenants of the space to communicate regularly, and that they should consult the bureau and the commission on their yearly plans as to the kinds of activities and arrangements they plan to put on,” Ho said.
Ho said that the creation of a new tender clause allowing the government to issue a fine if the number of event days held at the space fell short of commitments was due to the penalty never being clearly laid out in the past.
“But based on past experience, [meeting the required number of event days] should not be difficult to achieve,” he said.
In a statement on Thursday, the joint venture said that it would create synergies and showcase both sides of Victoria Harbour, and would submit detailed operational and execution plans to authorities.
It pledged to open the entire public space on days when there were no commercial events taking place. The venture is also set to pay the government HK$1.51 million (US$192,780) in monthly rent.

data.title: HK Electric to cut May fuel charges, but warns of sharp future increase
data.description:
HK Electric, the smaller of Hong Kong’s two electricity suppliers, will cut fuel surcharges for customers in May but has warned that costs could significantly rise later in the year due to the impact of the conflict in the Middle East.
The company said on Friday that its fuel clause charge for May will fall by 4.4 HK cents per kilowatt-hour to 26 HK cents per kWh, from 30.4 HK cents in April, citing a deferred effect under the monthly adjustment mechanism based on January’s average fuel costs. The decline marks the second consecutive monthly drop.
“Although international fuel prices have risen sharply since March amid geopolitical developments, the relatively lower fuel costs recorded in the first two months of the year, together with adjustments to the fuel mix and fuel-supply arrangements made in response to the Middle East’s situation, have provided a certain buffer for customers,” it said.
HK Electric supplies power to Hong Kong Island and Lamma Island, while its larger rival, CLP Power, serves Kowloon, the New Territories and Lantau Island.

Earlier this month, US President Donald Trump and Iran reached a temporary agreement to halt the conflict. However, the dispute has disrupted global energy supplies, including intermittent closures of the strategic Strait of Hormuz.
The fuel clause charge, a component of the overall tariff, reflects the actual cost of fuel used for power generation and is adjusted monthly.
As a result, the charge in May reflects relatively stable energy prices before the Middle East conflict, with higher fuel costs expected to feed through in the coming months.
“While the fuel clause charge for May has decreased due to the lower fuel costs earlier in the year, the impact of the sharp and substantial rise in fuel costs following the outbreak of the Middle East conflict will be unavoidable,” a HK Electric spokesperson said.
“These higher fuel costs are expected to take effect in the fuel clause charge starting from midyear, at which point the fuel clause charge will rise significantly.”
The spokesperson added that the company would continue to monitor energy market developments and fuel supply, while prudently managing fuel inventory, generation operations and statutory emission requirements to ensure a stable, reliable electricity supply.
Separately, CLP raised its fuel cost adjustment charge by 0.6 HK cents in April, to 39.8 HK cents per kWh.

data.title: How Intel is riding the ‘CPU comeback’ as AI shifts - and where China stands
data.description:
The central processing unit (CPU) – the chip technology that drove Intel’s sales and profits for decades but was overshadowed by the graphics processing unit (GPU) in the AI age – is making a comeback.
That’s according to Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan, speaking on the company’s latest earnings call. “The CPU is [reasserting] itself as the indispensable foundation of the AI era,” he said. “This isn’t just our wishful thinking, it’s what we hear from our customers.”
Intel shares rose about 20 per cent in after-hours trading on Thursday night local time, after it beat first-quarter earnings estimates, extending its rally since last year.
The chip giant, which appeared to lag in the early stages of the artificial intelligence boom, is showing signs of a rebound, mainly driven by surging demand for CPUs due to increasing AI agentic capabilities. Revenue from Intel’s data centre business rose 22 per cent to US$5.1 billion in the first quarter.
CPUs have returned to the spotlight in the era of agentic AI, marking a shift away from the earlier narrative of “more GPUs, more compute”.
The South China Morning Post explains the logic behind this “CPU renaissance” – and where China stands in design and manufacturing.
What is new in the CPU market now?
Intel, long the dominant player in CPUs, is regaining momentum after losing ground during the early, GPU-driven phase of the AI boom.
The company recently announced a multi-year collaboration with Google to deploy its Xeon processors – Intel’s data centre-focused CPUs – while expanding co-development of custom infrastructure chips.
The ramp-up of its 18A manufacturing node, alongside expanded production capacity in the US, has also underpinned its growth.
Nvidia has also renewed emphasis on its in-house CPU, Vera, as part of its Vera Rubin rack. “The CPU is no longer simply supporting the model; it’s driving it,” said Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang during his GTC 2026 keynote in March.
Rivals are also repositioning. Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) is scaling up its EPYC processors for AI-era workloads, while Arm Holdings is moving up the stack from architecture licensing to developing its own data centre chips. Together, they are competing for a larger share of AI infrastructure spending.

Why did CPUs fall out of favour, and why are they back?
GPUs are better suited to AI training and inference because they can run thousands of parallel operations simultaneously, making them far more efficient at handling the matrix calculations that underpin large language models. This sidelined CPUs in the race to develop AI models.
However, as AI evolves into more complex, agent-driven systems, these workloads require more orchestration, memory management and data movement – functions handled primarily by CPUs.
In a heterogeneous computing architecture, CPUs and GPUs are increasingly interdependent, with overall system efficiency determined as much by CPU capability as by accelerator performance.
Nvidia’s earlier Hopper AI server architectures often featured one CPU supporting more than a dozen GPUs. Nvidia’s Rubin platform, however, is moving closer to a one-to-two ratio.
In a recent note, Morgan Stanley estimated that CPU-side orchestration could account for 50 to 90 per cent of total system latency, and projected US$32.5 billion to US$60 billion in incremental CPU demand by 2030, driven by agentic AI.
What does the current supply-demand picture look like?
The prices of CPUs, especially those designed for AI data centres, are rising as surging demand collides with tight supply across the semiconductor supply chain.
Intel and AMD have reportedly informed customers of price increases of about 10 to 15 per cent, while lead times have lengthened significantly – from about one to two weeks previously to roughly eight to 12 weeks, a Nikkei report in March said.
Foundries are struggling to keep up with advanced-node demand, while chip packaging bottlenecks and lower yields add further strain. Foundry capacity is also being diverted to GPUs and memory chips, exacerbating shortages.
Constraints in key materials and equipment, alongside geopolitical disruptions, have pushed up costs across the industry.

Where does China stand in CPU design and manufacturing?
“China still lags leading players such as Intel and AMD, both in design and manufacturing,” said Joe Fei, a semiconductor analyst at LongBridge Securities, pointing to restrictions on access to advanced equipment such as extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines under US export controls.
On the design side, China is advancing along multiple tracks: improving performance in established ecosystems such as x86 and Arm – via AMD-derived designs at Hygon and in-house Arm cores at Huawei Technologies – while also pursuing greater independence through home-grown architectures like LoongArch and open-source RISC-V.
RISC-V is an open-standard instruction set architecture (ISA) that enables the development of CPUs under a shared software ecosystem. It became available for chip developers to configure and customise designs under the Switzerland-based non-profit RISC-V International in 2015.
Recent developments include Hygon’s next-generation server CPUs, Loongson chips showing competitiveness in select benchmarks, and Alibaba Group Holding unveiling a high-performance RISC-V processor aimed at AI workloads.
T-Head, Alibaba’s chip unit, in March revealed its Xuantie C950, a RISC-V processor that can be scaled by stacking multiple cores for data centre workloads and was claimed by the company as the most powerful of its kind globally.
Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

data.title: A ‘final piano song’ offers closure as residents say goodbye to Wang Fuk Court
data.description:
Playing one last song on a piano that cannot be retrieved from a fire-ravaged home is among the wishes of residents returning to the scene of the deadly Tai Po blaze in Hong Kong, with one man climbing stairs on a fractured leg to try and recover a water boiler linked to his childhood memories.
Some of those returning to their homes on Friday had lived in Wang Cheong House, where 81 of the 168 deaths occurred and 63 per cent of the flats were destroyed.
Only five floors of the block are being opened each day over a six-day period that ends on Tuesday.
Others are from Wang Yan House, where residents are returning in batches over three days until Saturday.
A Wang Cheong House resident surnamed Kwok suffered a fractured foot in the fire but was prepared to hobble up seven floors to his former home.
Kwok said he had asked a social worker whether he could return at a later date after his recovery but was told only that authorities would make proactive arrangements.

Fearing he might miss the opportunity, he decided to make the ascent with the help of friends and a folding chair.
“What I want to retrieve most is a water boiler, which I have had since I was a teenager,” Kwok said. “My father cleaned it several times with soda and lemon to keep it in good condition.”
Kwok’s father died a year ago, and he has been trying to recover items belonging to him. He said he particularly wanted to see the dining room, where his father used to sit and watch television.
A resident of Wang Yan House surnamed Woo, who lived on a middle floor, expressed deep fear about returning to her flat.
“I am so frightened to go back,” she said with tears, adding she would only return if accompanied by friends.
“But I hope to go up one last time to play a farewell song on the piano. This is my way of saying goodbye to my home.”
She said it was incredibly difficult to pack up almost her entire life’s belongings in just three hours.

Earlier, Mandy Wong, a resident of Wang Sun House, told the South China Morning Post that although her flat had blackened walls, scorched window frames and a peeling kitchen ceiling, her piano and sheet music remained intact.
The instrument’s red velvet cover retained its sheen and colour, standing out starkly against the charred surroundings, she added.
She also managed to retrieve her cello.
After packing, Kwok told the SCMP he had filled about 13 bags with belongings, adding he could hardly describe his emotions on returning home.
“It was unexpected. When I looked at the window where I used to wait for my father to come home when I was very young, I froze. I didn’t know how to describe my feelings,” he said.

He added that the flat was half-empty, as his father had died before the fire. Still, he managed to salvage several keepsakes, including a collection of vinyl records.
Kwok also considered taking his father’s old chest freezer. Because his father had mobility issues in his later years, the freezer had been essential for storing food.
However, given its size and his lack of storage space, he decided to leave it behind.
Ultimately, he could not find the water boiler and believed his father might have discarded it.
Under the 15-day programme that began on Monday, Wang Fuk Court residents are allowed three hours in their flats, excluding time spent on the stairs.
Over the past few days, residents have been seen carrying suitcases, nylon bags and cardboard boxes as they try to salvage appliances and keepsakes.

Each resident is accompanied by a police officer, with up to four people per household allowed to return at a time. However, only one or two may enter severely damaged flats deemed unsafe.
The inferno, which lasted about 43 hours and ravaged all but one block, was the deadliest in Hong Kong since 1948 and displaced nearly 5,000 people.

data.title: Huawei earmarks US$11.7b for training of autonomous-driving systems
data.description:

The massive capital expenditure would enhance the reliability of cars fitted with the Huawei Qiankun ADS autopilot system, as the Shenzhen-based tech giant looked to expand its customer base, said Jin Yuzhi, CEO of Huawei’s intelligent automotive solutions business unit.

data.title: China’s premium EVs mount tech-powered challenge to BMW, Mercedes-Benz at expo10-day show in Beijing, the world’s largest, to feature 1,451 models and exhibition area of 380,000 square metres.
data.description:
This year’s Auto China, the world’s largest car show, is likely to mark a watershed for Chinese electric vehicle (EV) makers as they push dozens of new premium models into the spotlight, challenging European manufacturers such as BMW and Mercedes-Benz.
Leading EV makers in mainland China, from BYD and Geely to Leapmotor and Nio, will showcase their latest models at the 10-day show, which starts in Beijing on Friday, having moved up the value chain through the rapid adoption of technological advances.
“Chinese EV brands will feel confident in promoting their new models priced above 300,000 yuan (US$43,978),” said Qian Kang, who runs a vehicle circuit board factory in Zhejiang province. “They will make the most of the Beijing auto show to convey the message to the global automotive industry and consumers around the world.”
The show’s organisers say 1,451 models will be displayed at the China International Exhibition Centre and the Capital International Exhibition Centre – the two venues for the event – until May 3. The exhibition area will be nearly 50 per cent bigger than it was at the previous show in 2024, hitting 380,000 square metres (4.09 million sq ft).
Among the vehicles to be exhibited in Beijing, 181 will be making their global debuts.
EVs priced below 100,000 yuan (US$14,647) have been a key growth driver for the Chinese car market since 2023, with subsidies helping to attract thousands of young and low-income customers.
About 7 million cars priced between 100,000 yuan and 150,000 yuan – including petrol-powered and electric models – were sold on the mainland in 2024, accounting for a third of the market by volume, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association.
However, since the middle of last year, Beijing’s efforts to discourage an EV discount war and instead push carmakers to focus on technological innovation to bolster sales have prompted a pivot away from low-priced models to premium ones.
Nick Lai, head of auto research for the Asia-Pacific region at JPMorgan, said carmakers were now taking one of two paths: upgrading production techniques to reduce manufacturing costs and make their prices more attractive, or offering consumers new models that outperformed existing ones at the same price.
Tian Maowei, a sales ­manager at Yiyou Auto Service in Shanghai, said: “Wealthy consumers took a cue from the government directives, hunting for value-for-money electric cars whose intelligent features were much more advanced than those of established luxury brands. All the big players are resorting to premium models to improve profitability now that the brutal price war has come to an end.”
German marques BMW and Mercedes-Benz dominated the mainland’s premium car segment over the past two decades, but BMW reported a 12.5 per cent fall in car sales on the mainland last year, while Mercedes-Benz saw its sales slump 19.5 per cent.
William Li Bin, CEO of Shanghai-based EV maker Nio, said last month that it had outsold BMW in Shanghai and other developed cities in the fourth quarter of last year.
Nio will promote its new fully electric ES9 SUV, which features its self-developed five-nanometre Shenji driving assistance chip, at the Beijing show. The SUV has a 102-kilowatt-hour battery pack that gives it a driving range of 620km (385 miles).
Aito, an EV brand backed by Huawei Technologies, will unveil its refreshed M9 SUV, a luxury vehicle fitted with six lidar sensors and a digital cockpit.
Shenzhen-based carmaker BYD’s second-generation Great Tang SUV will showcase its superfast charging technology, which the company says will ease drivers’ range anxiety by allowing them to drive 830km after nine minutes of charging.
Analysts said China’s carmakers and its companies in the automotive supply chain stand at the vanguard of EV technology and production, buoyed by government support and consumers’ willingness to embrace innovation.
On Friday, Hesai Group, the world’s largest maker of vehicle lidar sensors, introduced technology it said would raise autonomous driving features to a new level by detecting colours to improve the reliability of object identification.
On Tuesday, Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd, the world’s largest EV battery producer, announced that its sodium-ion batteries, an alternative to lithium-ion batteries, would be mass-produced and delivered in the fourth quarter of this year.
Sodium-ion technology uses a more widely available raw material and the batteries are cheaper to produce.

data.title: Tai Po probe: fire chief says authorities could have collaborated better
data.description:
At the start of the inquiry, committee lead counsel Victor Dawes identified six “human factors” that led to the blaze’s heavy death toll.
These included the use of flammable polyfoam boards to seal household windows, the use of allegedly non-fire-retardant scaffolding mesh, the wooden movable boards installed on emergency staircases that allowed easier access to scaffolding and replaced fireproof windows and workers’ smoking habits.
Other failures were the deactivation of the fire alarm system and the drained fire service tanks.
